title: Venice Film Festival Sees Sharp Drop in Female Directors in Competition, With May El-Toukhy Standing Solo
description: The Venice Film Festival has suffered its steepest decline in female representation in five years, with just one woman director selected for this ed
body:
The Venice Film Festival has suffered its steepest decline in female representation in five years, with just one woman director selected for this edition’s main competition. It marks a dramatic fall from the six female filmmakers in last year’s edition, which saw Kaouther Ben Hania win the grand jury prize for “The Voice of Hind Rajab” and Valérie Donzelli earn best screenplay for “At Work.”

	Danish-Egyptian director May El-Toukhy’s drama “Woman Unknown” is the sole female-directed film among the 20 titles competing for the Golden Lion at Venice this year, the lineup for which is curated by longtime chief Alberto Barbera.
	

	In recent editions, the official competition had maintained a relatively steady level of female representation. Last year, six of the festival’s 21 competition films were directed by women, including works by Mona Fastvold, Kathryn Bigelow, Enyedi Ildikó, Shu Qi, Donzelli and Ben Hania — making up 29% of the slate.

	In stark contrast with the competition, the Horizons section — also curated by Barbera and built around emerging voices and cutting edge works — will break its own record with eight out of 19 films directed by women, repping roughly 42% of the section. Out of these, at least two — Alina Marazzi with “La Ragazza Con La Leica” and Rubaiyat Hossain with “The Difficult Bride” — were understood to have been considered for a competition upgrade at some point. Anuparna Roy, who won the section’s best first film and director prizes last year with “Songs of the Forgotten Trees,” is also back with her sophomore outing, “Lovers in the Blue Night.”
	

	Elsewhere at the festival, Venice Days, the independently run sidebar that’s curated by Gaia Furrer, will actually boast a female majority with 14 films directed by women out of 25 titles. That lineup also opens with Hungarian filmmaker Lili Horvát’s English-language debut “My Notes on Mars,” starring Mackenzie Davis and Rupert Friend.

	Interestingly, a large proportion of female directors who vied for a Golden Lion within the last five years have taken home major awards. In 2021, Audrey Diwan won the Golden Lion for “Happening,” while Jane Campion took the Silver Lion for “The Power of the Dog” and Maggie Gyllenhaal won best screenplay for “The Lost Daughter.” The following year, Laura Poitras won the Golden Lion for “All the Beauty and the Bloodshed,” with Alice Diop taking the grand jury prize for “Saint Omer.” In 2023, Agnieszka Holland won the special jury prize for “Green Border.” 2024 saw a double win for women in the top prizes below the Golden Lion: Maura Delpero took the grand jury prize for “Vermiglio,” and Dea Kulumbegashvili won the special jury prize for “April.” And last year, Ben Hania and Donzelli won the grand jury prize and best screenplay, respectively.

	El-Toukhy’s “Woman Unknown” is already one of the anticipated highlights of the roster. The director comes to Venice with a prestige pedigree, having previously won Sundance’s Audience Award with her subversive film “Queen of Hearts,” about a successful lawyer who starts a dangerous affair with her teenage stepson. In “Woman Unknown,” a young housemaid guarding a shameful secret is about to marry the wealthy widower she works for.

	Yet, this year’s sharp drop in women directors in competition will likely reignite scrutiny over Venice’s record on gender representation. The festival was among the last major international film festivals to sign the 50/50 gender parity pledge in 2018. At the time, Barbera argued he would never select films simply because they were directed by women and has consistently rejected quotas, maintaining that the imbalance reflects the pool of submissions rather than the selection process. But the significant number of female directors elsewhere in the lineup raises questions about why so few made the jump into the main competition and reflects a deliberate editorial choice by Barbera.
	

	The contrast is particularly notable when compared with Cannes. While the French festival remains far from parity, it has maintained a considerably steadier level of female representation in competition over the past five editions, selecting five women-directed films in 2022, seven in 2023, four in 2024, a record seven in 2025 and five again this year. During that period, Cannes also awarded two Palme d’Or to women directors — Julia Ducournau with “Titane” and Justine Triet with “Anatomy of a Fall.”

schema.headline: Venice Film Festival Sees Sharp Drop in Female Directors in Competition, With May El-Toukhy Standing Solo

title: Tyler Perry’s ‘Beauty in Black’ Gets Surprise Season 4 Renewal After Final Season Announcement
description:
It turns out Tyler Perry‘s Netflix series “Beauty in Black” will be back for Season 4 after all. 

	Netflix has announced that the show is returning
body:
It turns out Tyler Perry‘s Netflix series “Beauty in Black” will be back for Season 4 after all. 

	Netflix has announced that the show is returning for a fourth season despite the fact that a third and final season was announced in December 2025. That season does not yet have a release date at the time of this publishing. 

	“’Beauty in Black’ is back for Season 4 and the Bellarie family’s story is far from finished,” Perry said. “There was so much more I wanted to explore and I’m grateful I get to keep telling this story. To the audience that has been on this ride with us from day one, thank you for watching and sharing it with the world. More episodes are on the way!”
	

	“Beauty in Black” originally premiered in 2024. The show’s two seasons to date have both been divided into two eight-episode batches each. It stars Taylor Polidore Williams, Crystle Stewart, Amber Reign Smith, Xavier Smalls, Ricco Ross, Julian Horton, Steven G. Norfleet, Richard Lawson, Terrell Carter, Bryan Tanaka, Charles Malik Whitfield, and Debbi Morgan. 

	The official logline for Season 3 states, “Kimmie (Polidore Williams) finally has a seat at the Bellarie table, but when a deadly family feud erupts, she’s forced into an uneasy alliance with Mallory (Stewart). Together, they navigate a ruthless world of blackmail, revenge, and buried secrets as the family wages war against one of its own, threatening to bring the entire empire crashing down.” 

	Additional cast members include: Bailey Tippen, Rodrigo Aburto, Randall J. Bacon, George Middlebrook, Greg Clarkson, Jasmine Burke, Aria Celeste Castillo, Gianmarco Duin, Philemon Chambers, Philip Boyd, Ty Anthony, Deeric Williams, Herb Magwood, Tre McBride, Kevin Savage, Jazmine Robinson, Kaja Brielle, Shay Mack, Steven Rho, Aaron Serotsky, Mikeal L Dwayne Griggs, Sara Spadacene, D’kia Anderson, Antoine Williams, Ty Courtad, Raven Chambers, Michael Butler, Jillmarie Lawrence, Jim Braswell
	

	Perry is the creator, writer, executive producer, and director of “Beauty in Black.” Angi Bones and Tony Strickland produce for Tyler Perry Studios, with music by Wow Jones & Jimijame$.

schema.headline: Tyler Perry’s ‘Beauty in Black’ Gets Surprise Season 4 Renewal After Final Season Announcement

title: Imax Second Quarter Earnings Rise as CEO Calls Christopher Nolan’s ‘The Odyssey’ a ‘Transformational Event’
description: Hits like “The Super Mario Galaxy Movie” and “Michael” helped lift Imax‘s second quarter earnings. During the three-month period ending in June, the
body:
Hits like “The Super Mario Galaxy Movie” and “Michael” helped lift Imax‘s second quarter earnings. During the three-month period ending in June, the big screen provider’s revenues increased 12% to $102.8 million, while net income improved 30% to $15.9 million. Imax recorded adjusted earnings per share of 43 cents, a 65% improvement over the prior-year quarterly results. 

	Imax’s global box office of $285 million marked the company’s highest second quarter results since 2019 and increased 1% year-over-year, which it said was driven by 24% box office growth in international markets excluding China. The top grossing titles in Imax over the three month period included “Michael” ($69 million), “The Mandalorian & Grogu” ($42 million) and “The Super Mario Galaxy Movie” ($39 million).
	

	But even as Imax touted its results, the company is already looking ahead to a monster third quarter, driven by the frenzy surrounding screenings of Christopher Nolan’s “The Odyssey” on the company’s screens. “The Odyssey” is the first film shot entirely with Imax cameras and Nolan’s advocacy of the format has led to sell-outs as theaters have scrambled to keep up with demand. Imax was responsible for 20% of the film’s global box office in its opening weekend, accounting for $52 million in sales. That’s a record for the company. Imax also reports it has $60 million in pre-sales for future showtimes of “The Odyssey.”


	In a statement, Imax CEO Rich Gelfond hailed “The Odyssey” as “a transformational event for Imax” as well as “the purest and most complete expression yet of the power of our global platform.”
	

	“The Odyssey has the potential to impact our business in many ways that are clear — and many ways we can’t yet predict, as its success on our platform reverberates across the creative community, and throughout the entertainment landscape,” Gelfond added. “In the near-term, the film gives us excellent momentum as we enter the second half of the year, with a strong slate that culminates with ‘Dune: Part Three,’ which was also shot with Imax film cameras and will be presented in Imax 70mm film.”

	During the second quarter, Imax installed 38 new systems compared to 36 systems in the same quarter in 2025.

schema.headline: Imax Second Quarter Earnings Rise as CEO Calls Christopher Nolan’s ‘The Odyssey’ a ‘Transformational Event’

title: International Cinema Union Criticizes Europe’s Approval of Paramount-Warner Bros. Merger: ‘It Has Based Its Decision on Too Narrow a Scope’
description: The International Union of Cinemas has criticized the European Commission’s approval of the Paramount-Warner Bros merger, saying the decision was ba
body:
The International Union of Cinemas has criticized the European Commission’s approval of the Paramount-Warner Bros merger, saying the decision was based on “too narrow a scope.”

	The union, known as UNIC, represents the European theater trade, including cinema associations and operators, across 39 territories.

	While UNIC welcomed the EC’s requirement for Paramount to cancel film distribution partnership with Universal in Europe in the next 13 months, its CEO Laura Houlgatte said “we strongly believe that the Commission could and should have gone further with its conditions for the merger’s approval.”
	

	“Our sector raised numerous concerns with its competition department about the proposed deal, and the Commission’s findings don’t reflect that bigger picture,” she added. “It has based its decision on too narrow a scope.”


	Among those risks, according to Houlgatte, are theatrical distribution arrangements outside the UIP territories as well as “theatrical windows, film diversity, preservation of film output and production pipelines, contractual practices and access to back catalogues.”

	“Given that the transaction continues to face legal challenges based on such factors in the United States, it is clear that these broader concerns about the merger remain unresolved and unanswered,” she said. “UNIC will keep a close eye on developments in the U.S. and any potential consequences for Europe.”

	UNIC was among the parties who provided evidence to the EC about the $110.9 billion deal and its potentially negative impact on the sector.
	

	Houlgatte noted that UNIC was also disappointed that independent advisory body European Media Board did not examine the “wider implications” of the merger and its knock-on effects on media pluralism, cultural diversity and the audiovisual market.

	The EC’s conditions of approval also saw Paramount forbidden from co-distributing films with Universal in the EAA and using Paramount’s distributor to distribute Warner Bros. films where it already distributes Universal or Disney films in certain countries, among other conditions.

schema.headline: International Cinema Union Criticizes Europe’s Approval of Paramount-Warner Bros. Merger: ‘It Has Based Its Decision on Too Narrow a Scope’

title: Iranian Director Mohsen Gharaei’s ‘Falling House’ Boarded by Paradise City Sales Ahead of Venice Premiere
description: Paradise City Sales has boarded worldwide sales on Iranian filmmaker Mohsen Gharaei’s latest feature, “Falling House” which is set to world premiere
body:
Paradise City Sales has boarded worldwide sales on Iranian filmmaker Mohsen Gharaei’s latest feature, “Falling House” which is set to world premiere in the Horizons section at the Venice Film Festival.

	“Falling House” marks his follow up to “Blockage,” a 2017 drama that portrayed contemporary Iranian society and won best film at Busan before going on to play at Rotterdam and Munich. 


	CAA Media Finance is handling U.S. rights, while Paradise City Sales represents international sales on “Falling House.”
	

	The film follows a modest family on the outskirts of Tehran who faces the imminent destruction of their home as part of a vast redevelopment project. While Rashid, the father and a prison guard, is being accused of corruption, he discovers that his eldest daughter, Ensi, is secretly planning to leave Iran to become a model. As the bulldozers draw ever closer, the tensions within the household begin to rise.


	“The most terrifying structures are the ones that no longer need a tyrant,” said Gharaei. “Their logic has already taken root in the minds of many. They become truly powerful the moment they are no longer recognized as violence. A prison no longer needs walls. It only needs people willing to call it home.”

	Written by Mohsen Gharaei and Amir Mohammad Abdi, “Falling House” stars Laleh Marzban, Rima Raminfar, Farhad Aslani and Elena Khakbaz. The film is scored by critically acclaimed composer Max Richter (“Hamnet”), cinematography by Farshad Mohammadi, editing by Evelyn Rack (“Sound of Falling”) and Emad Khodabakhsh, and sound design by Oscar-nominated Ansgar Frerich (“Of Fathers and Sons”).
	

	“Falling House” is produced by Milad Khosravi and Mohsen Gharaei for Seven Springs Pictures (“Divine Comedy”), Oscar-winning producer Odessa Rae for RaeFilm Studios (“The Voice of Hind Rajab”), and Daya Fernández, Amaury Nolasco and Alois Rubenbauer for 3SIX9 Studios. Basis Berlin Filmproduktion (“Of Fathers and Sons”) and Zentropa Germany (“King’s Land”) are co-producing, with Oscar-nominated producer Jon Kilik (“Babel,” “The Hunger Games”) serving as executive producer.

	“‘Falling House’ carries a warning we have felt growing for years,” said Khosravi, who is producing for Seven Springs Pictures. He added that the idea for the film “was conceived long ago, and the screenplay was completed before the present madness in our country could have been imagined.” “Yet today the film feels painfully prophetic, reflecting the long-term suffering of ordinary people who have endured decades of oppression and now face the fear and uncertainty brought by the ongoing war,” he continued. 

	Rae at RaeFilm Studios pointed “Falling House” is not a film about politics, but rather a “film about people living under its weight.” “Every system eventually arrives at someone’s front door. Mohsen captures this with extraordinary restraint,” she added.

	Jeanne Loriotti, sales executive at Paradise City Sales, said “Mohsen Gharaei has crafted a compelling, plot-driven story that combines a true sense of urgency with remarkably rich and layered characters.” She said the company was “struck by the film’s mounting tension, its emotional depth, and its remarkable performances, particularly from the female cast.” Mohsen Gharaei is represented by Seven Springs Pictures. 

	Paradise City Sales’ Venice slate also includes Andrea Pallaoro’s “The Echo Chamber” and Lili Horvát’s “My Notes on Mars,” opening Venice Days.

schema.headline: Iranian Director Mohsen Gharaei’s ‘Falling House’ Boarded by Paradise City Sales Ahead of Venice Premiere

title: Venice Horizons Opener ‘The Girl With the Leica’ Boarded by Fandango Sales (EXCLUSIVE)
description: Fandango Sales has boarded international sales on “The Girl With the Leica,” directed by Alina Marazzi, just announced by the Venice Film Festival a
body:
Fandango Sales has boarded international sales on “The Girl With the Leica,” directed by Alina Marazzi, just announced by the Venice Film Festival as the opening film of its Horizons section.

	The film paints a portrait of pioneering photojournalist Gerda Taro, the German-Jewish war photographer, anti-fascist and artist whose legacy has long been overshadowed. Together with her partner, the Hungarian photographer André Friedmann, Taro created the fictional photographer “Robert Capa” — a pseudonym adopted by Friedmann designed to overcome growing political intolerance in Europe and appeal to the American market. As a result, many of the photographs long attributed to Capa were in fact taken by Taro. While covering the Spanish Civil War, she became the first female photojournalist to be killed on the battlefield.
	

	“The Girl With the Leica” re-centers the story firmly on Taro herself — her work, her deeply held political beliefs, and the vibrant circle of admirers, artists and intellectuals drawn to her beauty, intelligence and independence.


	The film is set in Paris on July 14, 1937. Taro spends her final day before returning to the Spanish front with Capa, and the close friends with whom she escaped Nazi Germany. As past and present intertwine, the defining moments of her life resurface: her political activism in Leipzig alongside her first love, her years of exile in Paris, her whirlwind romance with Capa, her discovery of photography, and her growing determination to use her Leica as a tool for resistance and freedom.

	Raffaella Di Giulio, managing director, international sales and acquisitions for Fandango Sales, commented, “We are delighted to reunite with Vivo Film and to work for the first time with Alina. Through a beautiful and poetic blend of archival footage and fiction, she has crafted a stunning film that lets audiences rediscover a long-forgotten figure: Gerda Taro. We cannot wait to share her story with distributors and audiences alike.”
	

	Producers Marta Donzelli and Gregorio Paonessa for Vivo Film and Jonas Dornbach for Komplizen Film added: “Gerda Taro’s story feels even more urgent and relevant today than when we first began developing the project years ago. Through her work, she fought for freedom and never lost sight of the political power of photography, especially in times of war. Alina Marazzi is one of the most distinctive voices in Italian cinema. With this film she takes a remarkable step into fiction while remaining deeply true to what has always defined her work: an exploration of women’s lives, a creative incorporation of archival material, and a constant dialogue between history and the present.”

	The film features Emilia Schüle, Aaron Altaras, Luna Wedler, Lucas Englander and Thomas Schubert, along with Sebastian Schneider, Idan Weiss, Lia von Blarer, Hans-Christian Hegewald, Jeremy Mockridge and Joel Basman.

	Schüle, who stars as Taro, is best known for playing the lead role in the Canal+ and BBC Series “Marie Antoinette” and TV roles including Netflix’s “Der Parfumeur” and ZDF’s popular “Ku’damm” series. Her film credits include recent Berlinale breakout “The Trial of Hein,” as well as German box-office hits “Wunderschön” and “Wunderschöner.”

	“The Girl With the Leica” was lensed by Chilean-Danish cinematographer Manuel Alberto Claro, known for his Lars von Trier collaborations including “Melancholia” and “Nymphomaniac,” and integrates images shot by Taro as well as rarely seen archival material into the drama.

	The film is freely adapted from Helena Janeczek’s novel “The Girl With the Leica” (2017), recognized in Italy with the Premio Strega — widely regarded as Italy’s most prestigious literary award. The novel also won the Premio Bagutta, one of Italy’s oldest and most prestigious literary prizes, before being translated into several languages, including English, German, French and Spanish, and published in 15 countries.

	Marazzi is an acclaimed director working across documentary, fiction and theater, with women’s subjectivity and the imagery of memory at the heart of her work. Her films include “For One More Hour With You” (Un’ora sola ti vorrei), which received a Special Jury Mention at the 2002 Locarno Film Festival; “We Want Roses Too” (Vogliamo anche le rose), presented at Locarno and winner of the FOCAL Award in 2008; and “All About You” (Tutto parla di te), winner of the Tao Due – Caméra d’Or Award at the Rome Film Festival in 2012. She is also co-artistic director of the UnArchive Found Footage Fest.
	

	The screenplay was written by Marazzi and Valia Santella, freely adapted from the novel by Janeczek.

	“The Girl With the Leica” is an Italian-German co-production, produced by Marta Donzelli and Gregorio Paonessa for Italy’s Vivo Film with Rai Cinema, Jonas Dornbach, Janine Jackowski and Maren Ade for Germany’s Komplizen Film, in co-production with ZDF/ARTE, in association with Beatrice Bordone Bulgari for Eolo Film Productions, in co-production with Film i Väst and Zentropa Sweden, with the support of Eurimages – Council of Europe; MIC – Direzione Generale Cinema e Audiovisivo; with the contribution of PR FESR Piemonte 2021 – 2027 Bando Piemonte Film TV Fund; with the support of Film Commission Torino Piemonte; with the support of Avviso pubblico Lazio Cinema International (PR FESR Lazio 2021-2027); with the support of DFFF – Deutscher Filmförderfonds and MDM – Mitteldeutsche Medienförderung; with the support of Creative Europe Media.

	The film will be distributed in Italy by BIM Distribuzione with a release date to be announced and released in Germany by Port au Prince.

schema.headline: Venice Horizons Opener ‘The Girl With the Leica’ Boarded by Fandango Sales (EXCLUSIVE)

title: Prime Video Launches Games With Luna App Integration; ‘Tomb Raider,’ James Bond Titles Won’t Be in the Mix
description:
Amazon is bringing video games to Prime Video beginning Thursday with the launch of its cloud gaming service Luna within the app.

	Previously a sta
body:
Amazon is bringing video games to Prime Video beginning Thursday with the launch of its cloud gaming service Luna within the app.

	Previously a standalone app from Amazon available to Prime members, Luna is now also available on Prime Video on Fire TVs in the US and UK alongside the streamer’s movies, TV shows and sports. 

	Luna has a library of third-party games that includes “Hogwarts Legacy,” “EA Sports FC 26,” “Indiana Jones and the Great Circle” to Amazon’s “GameNight”-branded party games, like “Clue,” “Ticket to Ride,” and “Taboo.” Amazon’s gaming division has also developed its own additions, including the “Courtroom Chaos” games and co-op card battler “Masters of the Universe: Legends Unite.”
	

	“Prime members can find games the same way they discover everything else on Prime Video, just look for the new ‘Games’ tab and start playing in seconds right on your TV, using a controller or your phone,” the announcement from Amazon reads. “Luna is included with Prime at no additional cost and there’s nothing to download or purchase to start playing.”


	Luna will continue to be offered on its own, per Amazon gaming chief Jeff Gattis, for those who do not want to access games through Prime Video. At launch, the Premium tier of Luna (which costs $9.99 per month and can be purchased by non-Prime subscribers) will not be accessible through Prime Video, though that integration is part of the long-term plan.

	As for splashy titles owned by Amazon, like the two upcoming new “Tomb Raider” games (“Legacy of Atlantis” and “Catalyst”), and the future of the James Bond gaming franchise following the success of “007 First Light,” those games will not be included with Luna — for now. 
	

	Instead, there will likely be some sort of windowing strategy at play between when Amazon-published games are released for console and PC and when they could be launched on Luna.

	“So I don’t want to make news yet because we haven’t done this yet,” Gattis said. “But I think philosophically, in the way we think of it strategically, you’re absolutely right. I think a windowing strategy, not necessarily, I don’t want to commit to say it would be windowed only to Luna and exclusive. But maybe it goes into a Luna Premium catalog day one while it goes to PlayStation, Steam, and other platforms. There’s still a lot to work out on those fronts, but I’m sharing more of the vision and how we think about the business and the strategy. That’s it. I don’t think we want to take these games entirely exclusive, but we’ll do it on a case by case basis.”

	Gattis’ plan to move Luna into Prime Video began last year with the relaunch of the Luna app.

	“We saw a pretty significant uptick in our active player base that we’ve maintained,” Gattis said. “So we’ve got about 5x’d our number of active players. Now I’ll always caveat that’s on a small base, so we’re not taking a victory lap on that. But I do believe anytime you 5x anything,  it’s a signal that what we’re doing is resonating. And we saw really two things that were resonating with new audiences: that broad appeal, AAA, approachable, relatively simple game mechanics, like you see in a ‘Hogwarts Legacy’ or an ‘Indiana Jones and the Great Circle,’ and hopefully we’ll see in ‘Tomb Raider,’ all the way down through to those phone-as-a-controller, what we call ‘GameNight’ games that are really bringing people in for shorter sessions. Whether it’s together, couch co-op, and those kinds of things.”

	Gattis added: “But we also found some real resonance just around people like to have these 15-20 minute gameplay experiences. That was in some ways a learning for us, is that we maybe underestimated the single player attractability of some of those ‘GameNight’ games.”

	Despite the positive relaunch and a pop in the user base, Gattis knows Luna couldn’t grow much more until it was introduced to existing Prime customers through Prime Video.

	“The number one challenge we have, despite everything we did to push all that value into Amazon Prime, 200-million-plus Prime members worldwide, is not enough of them know that they have it,” Gattis said. “So it’s this weird place where we feel really good about the product, and we just have a general awareness and discoverability problem.”
	

	See more from Variety‘s interview with Gattis below.
	
		What made now the time to integrate Luna into Prime Video? Did Netflix’s recent releases of more games you can play on TV through the Netflix app affect this shift?	

	I think a couple things. The first one is that overall discoverability nature. So by putting it into Prime Video, we have the ability to literally have a games tab in the middle of Prime Video. You click, and all your Luna content will go there. So terms of just the viewership and usage of the Prime Video app compared to what we see on other services, it’s orders of magnitude more eyeballs, and so we do think there’s going to be a major push in terms of discoverability. 

	I continue to think of this this convergence that we’ve seen over the years between TV and movies and video games. Used to be, we’d see a movie made and a video game would get made maybe a few years later, and we’ve seen some vice versa, ‘Fallout’ being an example of that, but I think we’re starting to see a lot more crossover between these two mediums, and where they’re not really thought of as distinctive. Yeah, one is active and one is passive, but they’re still forms of entertainment. I think from a Prime Video standpoint, having a destination where all these forms of entertainment and engagement can happen on one platform, just seems like a customer-friendly thing to do. 

	Why do I have to go somewhere else when I’m done watching a TV show to go play video games? And I think Netflix sees a lot of that same kind of view too. Like, we’re an entertainment platform, maybe more than just a pure-play streaming video platform. So that was kind of the the impetus and interest inside of Amazon. The discussions with us in Prime Video and leadership was, yeah, now’s the time to do this. It certainly wasn’t a reactive statement to, Netflix is doing this and we need to go run at it. And in fact, a lot of these plans were in place before we really knew what Netflix was doing. But I think it’s both of us sort of reading the tea leaves on how customers are behaving and just moving hopefully in the direction that things are going.
	
		Were there discussions about limiting which games you make available through Prime Video — like only the party games vs. the AAA titles?	

	We obviously did talk about it. I have a kind of almost a religious conviction around not bifurcating catalogs. This goes back to my Xbox time as well. There were always discussions, what if we did this thing over here and we split out this? And I just think it creates a lot of customer confusion. And from a business standpoint, when we’re in the business of trying to explain to our customers why a certain thing is available here and not available there, it creates confusion. I’d say the other part, and this kind of goes back to the Netflix bit. And it to be clear, I don’t really see them as a competitor. We’ve kind of joked with them that I think we actually really want them to be successful. I think their success in many ways is our success, and vice versa. And I genuinely mean that because I think we versus the PlayStations and Xboxes of the world have a very different point of view on this. 
	

	Where we do have different approaches is [Amazon] still very much believes in controller-based AAA games. We think that those do have a place in the mainstream gaming discourse. We think that some of the tie-ins, a lot of those, a lot of those big IP movies and TV shows, are going to lend themselves to these big narrative-driven, storytelling-type AAA games. And so we’ve invested there. Netflix has gone kind of the other route. They believe in native mobile games as a way to bring people in. We haven’t done that yet, so I think there’s some differences in our approach. 

	But I think we both share the broader vision of trying to unlock audience that isn’t being reached today by the incumbents. And that problem is only, unfortunately for the market, getting worse when you start to think about a $1,000 Steam Machine, to start. Rumors of Xbox and PlayStations pushing $1,500- $2,000. It’s just a kind of a reality of our industry right now. But you can you fast forward a couple years– I was telling somebody this morning, a couple years ago when I was at Xbox, we had this Xbox Series S on discount for holiday. It was $190. Now you’re in a world three or four years later where we’re talking about $1,500. So I think at some point, cloud gaming has sort of been this thing, almost technology in search of a problem. I think the problem might be arriving. So our business is not predicated on moving console players immediately over to cloud or getting people to get rid of their gaming PCs. But also, hey, if that happens sooner, we certainly wouldn’t complain. 

	But that’s the part I worry about: mainstream players getting crowded out or just priced out of the market.
	
		Will the upcoming “Tomb Raider” games and future James Bond games be included with Luna?	

	No concrete announcements that we’re making here. I have said very publicly that the strategy is to really leverage our big IPs, the ones that make sense. “Tomb Raider,” we have the two games coming, and it’s tied to a TV show. That is sort of, from a content perspective, why we think Amazon makes sense here. So we look forward to four or five years from now where you think about Amazon, “Oh, that’s the place that has ‘Tomb Raider’ and these action-adventure games. Oh, there’s Bond and there’s the Bond films.” That is clearly the vision. 
	

	Specifically to Bond, because everybody asks, yes, we have the rights. We did not have the rights to this game [“007 First Light”]. IOI made the game. They made an awesome game. If you haven’t played it, I encourage you to do so. It was thoroughly fun. And so everybody asks, “What about a sequel?” I think obviously, given the success of the game, a sequel makes a ton of sense. We just haven’t gotten into those conversations yet with IOI or anybody else, for that matter. I always say, trust that we’re going to do right by the game. If we’re going to make another game, it’s going to be a fun game. We’re going to pick the right people to make it.
	
		How are you seeing the breakdown between Luna’s regular player base of activated Prime members vs. the Premium tier of paid Luna subscribers?	

	The player growth is largely coming through Prime, and that’s good, because that’s the strategy. That’s why we’re doing this and we want to continue to do that and really use that as our growth engine. But the goal is to move a lot more people up to Premium. Obviously, we need to continue to invest in Premium and create a real value proposition where people move up the stack. Not surprisingly, the people that do pay for Premium are engaged more every month, more daily active users, more hours per month. That kind of checks out with what you’d expect. But our general philosophy is, leverage Prime as our growth engine to bring people in, move a certain percentage of those people into the Premium SKU. That’s sort of how the long-term business model works for Amazon. The goal of this is to build a sustainable gaming business for Amazon that is material to our P&L. It’s not to just be another Prime benefit.

	Watch the new promo video showcasing how Luna games will look on Prime Video below.

schema.headline: Prime Video Launches Games With Luna App Integration; ‘Tomb Raider,’ James Bond Titles Won’t Be in the Mix

title: ‘The Dink’ Review: Pickleball Takes on Tennis in a Mild Underdog Sports Comedy
description: You don’t have to hit hard to win pickleball — that’s the chief lesson to be taken from “The Dink,” a sports comedy titled for the dainty signature
body:
You don’t have to hit hard to win pickleball — that’s the chief lesson to be taken from “The Dink,” a sports comedy titled for the dainty signature shot of the fast-growing racket sport, and it’s one that Josh Greenbaum‘s film takes to heart with its own softball comic style. Following a conceited former tennis prodigy as he’s taught a lesson in humility by a bunch of enthusiastic amateur pickleballers, this amiable-enough Apple TV original aims for the non-confrontational good vibes that most associate with the newer game, while also presenting its players as fiercer competitors than you might think. 
	

	The presence of Ben Stiller as producer (and in an oddly flat supporting role) underlines a parallel with 2004’s “Dodgeball: A True Underdog Story,” which likewise poked gentle fun at a sport that fundamentally cannot be made to look cool on screen. But where that film was often raucously funny in its knockabout silliness, throwing out gags so fast it didn’t matter if a lot of them missed, “The Dink” has considerably less frenetic energy, or energy at all really. 


	More cheerfully lightweight than it is actively funny, the film is mostly content to coast on the affable hangdog charm of “New Girl” star Jake Johnson — who’s a commendably likable anchor for proceedings considering his character is, at least half the time, a blundering douchebag. He’s bolstered by a classy ensemble of supporting players who are uniformly under-challenged (save perhaps for good-sport tennis pros Andy Roddick and John McEnroe as themselves), but at least a perma-glowing Mary Steenburgen gets the most to do as his not-quite-romantic foil.
	

	Dangled all too briefly, the possibility of a convention-flipping love affair between the 48-year-old Johnson and 73-year-old Steenburgen — who do, let it be said, have endearingly sparky chemistry — is pretty much the only surprise on offer in an otherwise strictly formulaic script by “Hollywood Handbook” podcast host Sean Clements, making his feature writing debut. You know where it’s going the minute Dusty Boyd (Johnson), working as a tennis coach at the elite racket club run by by his tough-as-nails dad Chuck (Ed Harris), bawls out senior-citizen pickleballer Candace (Steenburgen) when her errant ball lands on his court, venting his resentment of a sport he and his father see as beneath them.

	Dusty has an awful lot of resentment to vent, in fact. A former teenage tennis prodigy who was aggressively coached by his dad to become a champion, he lost that dream to injury in a juniors match against Roddick years ago, and neither he nor the eternally unforgiving Chuck has ever got over the disappointment. These days, only the club’s awkward, unathletic Chinese-Australian underling PJ (Aaron Chen) looks up to him. A highly contrived scenario sees the club’s tennis and pickleball factions fighting for more court space, necessitating a deciding duel between their star players: Mortified not to be automatically picked by his father to represent their side, Dusty proves his mettle in a qualifying game, only for his old injury to flare up again.

	His quirky physician (Stiller, burdened with a mirthless running joke about sex apps) suggests he take up pickleball while he recovers, which is how Dusty is improbably drafted into a league populated primarily with women of retirement age — including Candace, who graciously accepts him as her doubles partner. Naturally, he finds the sport more difficult than he expected, and eventually, as he gains in ability, more fun too. Where does this leave him regarding his loyalties to his father? Well, who cares? The stakes in “The Dink” never rise above the height of a pickleball net, and that extends to his burgeoning friendship with Candace, which he initially mistakes for a May-December (or perhaps closer to a June-November) attraction.

	The growingly tender dynamic between between this cocky not-so-young buck and a careworn older woman is the most interesting thing “The Dink” has going for it, and could rewardingly be the focus of a less broad, more character-driven comedy. Instead, Greenbaum (“Barb and Star Go to Vista Del Mar”) and Clements skip around between a couple of shticky subplots and comic sideshows: Chen is the butt of the cheapest comedy here as a fawning, sexless lackey, while Harris, amusing in maximum gruff mode, is handed the most unconvicing redemption arc.
	

	It’s an easy enough watch, though it never surpasses sitcom scale in tone or form: Greenbaum and editor Greg Hayden largely pass up the opportunity to make pickleball play either cinematically or comically in the match scenes. In fact, it’s tennis that gets the best joke here, in McEnroe’s self-aware parody of his own commentary style. It’s witty, but one can’t help feeling that “The Dink” needs bigger laughs, and bigger swings.

schema.headline: ‘The Dink’ Review: Pickleball Takes on Tennis in a Mild Underdog Sports Comedy

title: BritBox’s ‘Gone’ Is a Masterfully Tense Crime Drama: TV Review
description: Writer George Kay certainly knows his way around a mystery. The success of his Netflix series “Lupin” and his Apple TV drama “Hijack” (especially th
body:
Writer George Kay certainly knows his way around a mystery. The success of his Netflix series “Lupin” and his Apple TV drama “Hijack” (especially the first season) have well proven his abilities. With his latest crime drama, BritBox’s “Gone,” loosely inspired by Julie Mackay and Robert Murphy’s true crime novel, “To Hunt A Killer,” Kay further proves that he has mastered suspense and the art of building tension. Though it starts as a mystery drama, “Gone” slowly unfurls, becoming a tale about obsession, emotional ineptness and the horrific things men do when they aren’t given what they want.​“Gone” is set in Bristol, England, and begins at a rugby match for the prestigious St. Bartholomew private school. As his team scores the winning goal, St. Bartholomew headmaster and coach Michael Polly (a haunting David Morrissey) looks over the field stiff and stonefaced. Despite the joyous celebration happening in front of him, he never even cracks a smile. When the match ends, Michael and his 25-year-old daughter, Alana (Emma Appleton), who is also a teacher at St. Bartholomew, head home after he berates star player Dylan Sedgwick (Billy Barratt) for what he deems unsporting behavior. For decades, they’ve lived on school grounds in the home allotted for the headmaster and his family.​Yet this isn’t a normal evening. Though her reading glasses and a cold cup of tea sit in her office, Michael’s wife Sarah (Hollie Bailey), a music teacher, is nowhere to be found. Her phone continuously goes to voicemail. When she still hasn’t turned up the next morning, Alana panics and urges her father to report her mother missing. Detective Sargents Annie Cassidy (Eve Myles) and Becky Hammond (Jennifer Macbeth) first encounter Michael when he arrives at the Redland Police Station to inform them of Sarah’s disappearance. Though the frigid headmaster answers their questions about his wife and his own timeline without flair, his demeanor immediately puts Annie on edge. It’s clear Michael is hiding something.​Not wanting to raise the alarm yet, Annie and Becky label Sarah as a low-risk missing person and head to St. Barths to begin a more formal interview process with Michael and Alana. Before she can fully untangle Sarah’s disappearance, Annie is forced into a new role as the family’s liaison, watching father and daughter around the clock while trying to piece together what may have happened to Sarah. As the hours tick away, Alana continues to emotionally unravel while Michael retreats inward, stonewalling law enforcement and his staff and pressing forward with life as usual.​Despite being relegated to the duty of glorified babysitter, Annie starts to put together a portrait of the Pollys. She quickly understands that the image projected to others is very different from the truth of who they really are. Because their lives are so deeply embedded in the school, other things also come into focus, including the tension between Michael and Dylan and the secret romance Alana is having with fellow professor Paul Whitchurch (Nicholas Nunn). Meanwhile, though Annie feels sidelined on the case, she is not at the top of her game. A missing friend’s 20-year-old cold case continues to haunt her, and she’s distracted by her estranged husband, Detective Inspector Craig Stanhope’s (Peter McDonald) reemergence in her life. Though he’s showing signs that he’s just as volatile as he always was, Annie can’t seem to resist rekindling their romance.​In just six episodes, “Gone” reaches a resolution, and the audience finds out what happened to Sarah Polly. But the series is careful to cast suspicions in a multitude of directions, not merely at Michael but at several people in Sarah’s orbit who only reveal themselves when backed into corners. Bombshells are exposed bit by bit, and the pacing keeps viewers holding their breath for most of the show. As Annie gets closer to the truth, she realizes violence often has many different faces, and typically the more insidious it is, the more horrific the outcome.
	

	The first three episodes of “Gone” debut July 23 on BritBox, with the remaining episodes airing weekly on Thursdays.

schema.headline: BritBox’s ‘Gone’ Is a Masterfully Tense Crime Drama: TV Review

title: ‘George Michael: The Faith Tour’ Concert Tour Film Acquired by Bleecker Street, Universal Pictures Content Group
description: The concert film “George Michael: The Faith Tour” has been acquired by Universal Pictures Content Group and Bleecker Street’s event cinema division
body:
The concert film “George Michael: The Faith Tour” has been acquired by Universal Pictures Content Group and Bleecker Street’s event cinema division Crosswalk.

	Universal Pictures Content Group will release the film internationally, and Bleecker Street’s Crosswalk will release the film in North America.

	Shot at the Palais Omnisports de Paris-Bercy during the European leg of the Faith Tour in1988, the film features never-before-seen footage of Michael. 
	

	The concert film is directed by Michael’s longtime collaborators Andy Morahan (“Last Christmas”; “Faith”; “Careless Whisper”) and David Austin (George Michael “Freedom Uncut”, “George Michael at the Palais Garnier, Paris”). Screening with an opening original short film by Mary McCartney (“If These Walls Could Sing; “Mary McCartney Serves It Up!”) and produced by George Michael Entertainment and Globe Originals, in partnership with Mercury Studios, the film is slated for a late 2026 worldwide release in theaters in premium formats. In the U.S., Bleecker Street will partner with Imax on exclusive earlyaccess screenings.


	“George Michael: The Faith Tour” transforms archival footage assembled from a 14-camera shoot captured on 35mm film over two nights, into a cinematic event and audio experience. The film opens with an original short, “Finding Faith,” which features voice-over from Michael during an unheard interview alongside unseen images captured by photographer Herb Ritts, and behind-the-scenes footage of the “Faith” music video.

	The deal was negotiated by Helen Parker for Universal Pictures Content Group and Avy Eschenasy for Bleecker Street.
	

	Parker said, “George at the height of the Faith tour was simply electrifying — there’s a magnetic force of charisma and connection with the audience that feels just as powerful today as it did then.” She added, “To see these performances brought to life on the big screen, in such a cinematic and immersive way,makes this incredibly special — particularly for those who were there or remember that moment so vividly. We also can’t wait for a whole new generation to experience just how extraordinary he was as a creative force, performer and musical auteur.”

	Bleecker Street CEO Kent Sanderson said, “An icon of his generation and beyond, George Michael was at the height of his creative and cultural power during the Faith tour, a defining moment that was made for the big screen. Through beautifully restored archival footage and intimate, never-before-heard interviews, audiences will be given a front-row seat to a beloved artist at the peak of his impact.”

	Hit singer Michael, the founding member of the duo Wham!, died at the age of 53 in 2016. The singer sold more than 100 million albums over a career spanning almost four decades.

	“We are incredibly proud of ‘George Michael: The Faith Tour’ and the extraordinary craftsmanship that has gone into bringing this footage to life,” said Ken Danquah, Chief Finance & Commercial Officer, Mercury Studios. “We’re excited to be partnering with such world-class distributors as Universal Pictures Content Group and Crosswalk, whose proven track record and expertise in delivering immersive cinematic events make them the perfect partners to bring this film to audiences around the world.”

	The London-born singer, whose real name was Georgios Kyriacos Panayiotou, formed Wham! in 1981 with school friend Andrew Ridgeley, and the pop duo went on to have a string of hits, including “Wake Me Up Before You Go-Go,” “Club Tropicana” and “Last Christmas.”

	Michael and Ridgeley parted ways in 1986, and the former embarked on a solo career. His first album, “Faith,” sold more than 25 million copies, and won a Grammy. 

	“George was utterly unique, both as a man and an artist. Having had the great privilege of personally working closely with him and his team over the years, the opportunity to bring The Faith Tour to audiences through Globe Originals is an honor,” said Lucie Avery, general manager, Globe Originals. “Mercury Studios, working hand-in-hand with George Michael Entertainment, brought their unrivaled expertise and vision to this project from the outset. The result is a film that truly honors his timeless legacy.”
	

	Alongside the theatrical release, George Michael Entertainment and Sony Music Entertainment will release “The Faith Tour”, an 18-track live album of previously unreleased recordings featuring hits from Michael’s Wham! and solo catalogs.

schema.headline: ‘George Michael: The Faith Tour’ Concert Tour Film Acquired by Bleecker Street, Universal Pictures Content Group

title: Joe Rogan Says He Interviewed Man Who Allegedly Cut Someone's Head Off
description:
Joe Rogan
                          I Once Interviewed A Head-Chopping Maniac!!
body:
Joe Rogan
                          I Once Interviewed A Head-Chopping Maniac!!
                      
                  
                                                                        Published 
                              July 23, 2026
                            6:01 AM PDT
                                                                            
      
    
        
                    
                  
      
    
            Joe Rogan has made a stunning -- and gruesome -- revelation, saying he once interviewed a guy who later chopped off somebody's head!
      
    
            The popular podcaster revealed the shocking tidbit while chatting with physician Zach Bush during Wednesday's episode of the "Joe Rogan Experience."
      
    
            Joe kicked things off by stating, "We had a guy in this studio that cut a guy’s head off after the podcast."
      
    
            Rogan added that the guy had a beef with the victim before killing him, decapitating him and putting his body in a suitcase.
      

                  
          
                          
                
                  
      
    
            Joe said the alleged killer then put on a wig and tried to escape through a hallway but was captured on surveillance video.   
      
    
            Joe was referring to Sheldon Johnson, a criminal justice activist who appeared on Rogan's podcast in 2024 to talk about his rehabilitation after getting out of prison for committing a bunch of robberies.
      
    
            One month after the podcast, Johnson allegedly shot to death Colin Small and sawed off his head, placing it inside a freezer at Small's apartment in The Bronx, NYC.
      
    
                  
          
                          
                
                  
      
    
            Neighbors called police after seeing someone coming and going from the apartment with cleaning products.
      
    
            Cops found Small's body parts -- his arms, legs and head -- inside the apartment and then reviewed the building's video surveillance, which allegedly caught Johnson in the act. 
      
    
            Johnson was eventually arrested and charged with murder, manslaughter, and criminal possession of a weapon.

title: Secret Service Agent Reportedly Sidelined For Leaking J.D. Vance's Travel Plans
description:
Secret Service Agent
                          Allegedly Put On Leave ...
body:
Secret Service Agent
                          Allegedly Put On Leave ...
                          After J.D. Vance Travel Leak
                      
                  
                                                                        Published 
                              July 23, 2026
                            4:06 AM PDT
                                                                      |
                                                                Updated 
                                  July 23, 2026
                                6:28 AM PDT
                                    
      
    
                  
          
                          
                
                  
      
    
          UPDATE
  
    
            7/23/20026 6:24 AM PT ... A Secret Service official tells TMZ ... A member of the Vice Presidential Protective Division is the subject of an administrative investigation, and potential criminal inquiry, involving allegations of compromising operational and information security." The official adds, "One principle is unequivocal: any conduct that potentially threatens the potential safety of a protectee will not be tolerated."
      
    
            A Secret Service agent is in hot water with J.D. Vance after allegedly leaking info to reporters about the VP's travel habits.
      
    
            The agent, who was not identified, was placed on administrative leave pending the outcome of an internal review by federal investigators into the suspected leak to MS NOW correspondents, according to CNN. 
      
    
                  
          
                          
                
                  
      
    
            The internal probe stems from the agent allegedly passing information to MS NOW for a July 15 story about members of Vance's security detail being pissed off that they have to provide coverage for the Veep's personal travel schedule, calling it a burden.
      

            The MS NOW story laid out Vance's travel plans in detail -- and even revealed that the VP was going to use a Marine Corps helicopter to fly his son to a golf lesson, but the flight was eventually scrapped.
      
    
            CNN says MS NOW's report infuriated top officials in the Secret Service, FBI, and the White House ... who were angry that operational details leaked to the press.
      
    
            We've reached out to the Secret Service for comment ... so far, no word back.
      
      
              
            
              Related articles

                Travel

schema.headline: Secret Service Agent Reportedly Sidelined For Leaking J.D. Vance's Travel Plans
